episodes.title: David Friedman - Dating Markets, Legal Systems, Bitcoin, and Automation
episodes.guest_title: Economist and Author
episodes.summary: Friedman discusses the dating market, arguing that it is inefficient because individuals are not substitutable, and potential dates have different tastes. He also argues that legalizing polygyny would benefit women by raising the price of a wife. He also discusses the difficulty of enforcing contracts in a world of strong privacy, and the importance of Schelling points in coordinating behavior.
key_highlights: Online dating is inefficient because it is difficult to sort people by taste
Legalizing polygyny would benefit women by raising the price of a wife
The effect of polygyny is the opposite of what people expect
Search costs are central to the dating problem
The marriage market is not a competitive market
The effect of polyandry is the opposite of what people expect
The effect of polygyny is to benefit women and harm men
episodes.quotes: If men can have multiple wives, that's going to bid up the price of a wife. It means that some men can in effect go on the market and try to bid for two wives instead of one.

episodes.title: NotOnTheHighStreet.com Founder: Rapid Success Lead To My Darkest Days - Holly Tucker | E92
episodes.guest_title: Founder of NotOnTheHighStreet.com and Holly & Co.
episodes.summary: Holly Tucker recounts the meteoric rise of NotOnTheHighStreet.com, detailing the entrepreneurial journey from its inception to rapid success. She candidly shares how this intense period of business growth led to her 'darkest days,' causing her to lose personal orientation and identity within the company. Tucker discusses her decision to leave the successful venture to embark on a path of self-rediscovery and now champions the concept of building 'good-life' businesses. Her current work focuses on helping others create enterprises that align with personal well-being, emphasizing the importance of passion and purpose in one's work.
key_highlights: Rapid entrepreneurial success can paradoxically lead to personal disorientation
The journey of building NotOnTheHighStreet.com from concept to major success
The critical importance of strategic hiring for sustainable business growth
Losing personal identity and self within an all-consuming business venture
The necessity of stepping away to rediscover oneself and personal purpose
Advocating for creating businesses that genuinely support a 'good life'
Emphasizing the profound value of aligning work with personal passion
episodes.quotes: I lost myself within the business, and rediscovering who I was became my most important journey.

episodes.title: Patricia Bright: How She Made Her Millions | E91
episodes.guest_title: Content Creator, Entrepreneur, and Influencer
episodes.summary: Patricia Bright discusses her journey from early feelings of displacement to successfully transitioning a hobby into a multi-million-dollar career. She shares insights on building confidence for career shifts and navigating the unique challenges faced by women in the influencer space. The episode delves into her new financial projects, the dynamics of working with her husband, and coping with the darker side of public life, including a stalker. Strategically transforming a personal hobby into a profitable career
Navigating the specific pressures and opportunities for women influencers
Expanding into new financial ventures and business projects
Managing a successful professional partnership with her husband

episodes.guest_title: Award-winning Broadcaster, Filmmaker, and Actor

The discussion also explores his evolving relationship with money, the profound influence of both romantic and parental relationships, and the essential nature of authenticity in defining and achieving one's unique potential. He reflects on key moments from his filmmaking career and his vision for the future.

episodes.guest_title: Podcaster, YouTuber, and Content Creator for True Geordie

The conversation explores his parents' influence, the role of fitness in his mental health recovery, and his strategic decision to step back from podcasting and comments to prioritize his mental rebuild.
key_highlights: True Geordie's early life and family influences shaped his ambition
The dramatic rise and subsequent fall of his online persona
Candid discussion of personal struggles with anxiety and depression
The challenge of attaching happiness solely to future goals
Fitness as a critical tool for managing mental health
Strategic decision to pause podcasting and disable comments for well-being
Reflecting on ambition, self-worth, and the elusive nature of happiness

episodes.title: Mary Portas: How To Stop Living A Life That Isn't True To You | E85
episodes.guest_title: Retail and Brand Consultant, Author, and Advocate for the Kindness Economy
episodes.summary: This episode delves into Mary Portas's personal journey of self-discovery, emphasizing the critical role of authenticity and intuition in life. She shares how early experiences and painful moments shaped her identity, leading to a period of feeling lost before embracing her true self, including her sexuality. A significant part of the discussion focuses on her advocacy for a 'Kindness Economy,' detailing what it means for businesses to operate with greater empathy and purpose. Portas offers advice for younger generations on navigating self-discovery and discusses the challenges of balancing personal relationships with demanding business careers.

Trusting intuition is key to authentic living
Embracing true self, including sexuality, brings peace
Advocating for a 'Kindness Economy' in business
Navigating relationships amidst business demands
Advice for younger generations on self-discovery
Overcoming feeling lost to find one's true path
episodes.quotes: Living a life true to you means listening to your intuition and bringing kindness into every aspect, from personal choices to how we build businesses.

Diversity fosters innovation and team effectiveness
Understanding psychological barriers to potential
Cultivating confidence and self-belief for leadership

episodes.title: Sarah Fitz-Claridge - Taking Children Seriously | The Lunar Society #15
episodes.guest_title: Writer, Coach, and Speaker
episodes.summary: Sarah Fitz-Claridge discusses her educational philosophy of "Taking Children Seriously," which emphasizes treating children as rational, creative individuals capable of making their own choices. She argues against coercion in education and parenting, advocating for creating consent and solving problems collaboratively. She contends that traditional schooling and authoritarian parenting stifle children's natural curiosity and problem-solving abilities, leading to resentment and a lack of self-trust.
key_highlights: Children should learn what they want, what interests them
Children should learn how to solve problems
Institutionalization for 12 years is not the way to learn
Coercion decides issues under an irrational institution
Might makes right is false
Children are creative and rational from birth
episodes.quotes: "What children should be learning is what they want, is what interests them, is how to solve problems. They don't learn that by being institutionalized for 12 years and bossed about by an authoritarian teacher who doesn't know very much."

episodes.title: Johann Hari: Everything You Think You Know About Meaning & Happiness Is Wrong | E82
episodes.guest_title: Author and Journalist
episodes.summary: This episode challenges conventional wisdom about meaning and happiness, arguing that modern society's approach is fundamentally flawed. Johann Hari discusses the 'Rat Park' experiment, illustrating how environment and connection, not just substances, drive addiction and well-being. He explores how pervasive screen time, working from home, and social media contribute to a sense of digital isolation and superficial connections, hindering our ability to find genuine meaning. The conversation delves into the struggle to form deep human bonds and the potential role of psychedelics in fostering connection and understanding.
key_highlights: Rat Park experiment challenges traditional views on addiction and environment
Screen-based living and remote work can erode genuine human connection
Finding meaning requires deeper engagement beyond the superficial modern 'machine'
Modern society struggles significantly with forming truly meaningful, deep human connections
Social media's role in collective action versus individual isolation is complex
Psychedelics are explored as a potential tool for fostering connection and understanding
episodes.quotes: Our modern pursuit of happiness often misdirects us, leading away from true meaning and the vital connections we desperately need.

episodes.title: Michael Huemer - Anarchy, Capitalism, and Progress
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Philosophy, University of Colorado
episodes.summary: Michael Huemer discusses the problem of political authority, arguing that the state's claim to authority is not self-evident and requires justification. He argues that common sense morality and ethical intuitions should be applied to the state. Huemer suggests that the state's authority is often based on a combination of fear, Stockholm syndrome, and a bias towards the status quo. He also discusses the potential for technology to drive moral progress, particularly in areas like animal welfare.
key_highlights: The state's claim to authority is not self-evident and requires justification
Common sense morality should be applied to the state
The state's authority is based on fear, Stockholm syndrome, and status quo bias
Moral progress is driven by a small number of people
Technology can drive moral progress, especially in animal welfare
episodes.quotes: "The law could be things that you're not already obligated to do."

episodes.guest_title: Sleep Physiologist and Founder, Sleepyhead Clinic
episodes.summary: Stephanie Romiszewski debunks common sleep misconceptions, highlighting the 'bullshit' surrounding popular sleep advice and offering evidence-based strategies. She provides practical tips for designing an optimal sleep environment, managing factors like caffeine and diet, and understanding the impact of the snooze button. The episode also explores the significant correlation between poor mental health and sleep issues, discussing how the pandemic has exacerbated these problems and offering insights into the therapy available for sleep disorders. Ultimately, she aims to empower listeners with actionable steps to improve their sleep tonight.
key_highlights: Debunking common sleep misconceptions and 'bullshit' advice
Designing an optimal sleep environment for better rest
Immediate, practical tips to improve sleep quality tonight
Understanding the impact of caffeine, sleeping pills, and diet on sleep
Exploring the significant link between poor mental health and sleep
Identifying characteristics distinguishing good sleepers from bad sleepers
The pandemic's profound impact on global sleep patterns and challenges
episodes.quotes: Forget the common myths; the secret to a good night's sleep lies in understanding your unique physiology and implementing practical, consistent habits.

episodes.title: The productivity Hack I Use Everyday (part 2) | E63
episodes.guest_title: Host and Founder, The Diary of a CEO
episodes.summary: This episode, a continuation from part 1, reflects on the challenging year of 2020 and extracts valuable life lessons for improving one's outlook in 2021. Steven Bartlett emphasizes the transformative power of consistency and the importance of cultivating optimism and proactivity. He discusses the critical insight that being easily provoked leads to being easily controlled, highlighting the need for self-respect and identifying one's most dangerous flaws. Bartlett also advises listeners to resist external labels and underscores the fundamental value of hard work in achieving personal growth.
key_highlights: Embrace consistency for transformative personal growth
Cultivate optimism and proactivity to navigate life's challenges
Emotional control prevents external manipulation and control
Prioritize self-respect as a foundation for personal strength
Identify and address your most dangerous personal flaws
Resist external labels to define your own identity
Recognize the indispensable value of diligent hard work
episodes.quotes: If you are easily provoked you are easily controlled.

episodes.title: The Productivity Hack I Use Everyday (part 1) | E62
episodes.guest_title: Host of The Diary of a CEO podcast
episodes.summary: In this self-reflection episode, host Steven Bartlett looks back at 2020, a year he describes as difficult and heartbreaking, to share the biggest lessons he learned. He emphasizes the importance of learning to live fully despite life's challenges, suggesting that hardship reveals more about ourselves. Bartlett unveils a specific productivity hack he adopted during 2020 and discusses the strategy of creating a sense of urgency. The episode aims to provide insights and actionable advice for listeners to make 2021 a better and happier year.
key_highlights: Reflecting on the biggest lessons learned from the difficult year 2020
Emphasizing the importance of learning to live fully despite hardships
Unveiling a specific productivity hack discovered and used in 2020
Discussing the strategy of creating a powerful sense of urgency
Strategies for making the upcoming year, 2021, better and happier
Self-reflection on personal growth and resilience during challenging times
episodes.quotes: Within the hard times of life we tend to learn more about ourselves and who we are.

episodes.title: World Leading Psychologist: How To Succeed In Life & World: Jamil Qureshi
episodes.guest_title: High Performance Psychologist & Expert
episodes.summary: Jamil Qureshi, a high-performance psychologist, shares the secrets behind unlocking individual potential and mastering the mind. The episode explores the strategies employed by top businesses, elite sports teams, and successful individuals to achieve peak performance. Listeners will discover hidden insights into their success and learn how to apply these powerful techniques to their own lives. Qureshi reveals actionable methods for reaching the highest level of personal and professional achievement.
key_highlights: Unlocking personal potential for success
Mastering the mind for peak performance
Secrets of high-achieving individuals
Strategies for reaching elite levels
Applying expert insights to your life
Techniques for mental mastery
episodes.quotes: Unlock your own potential and master your mind.

episodes.title: Addiction, Childhood Trauma And Depression With Joe Wicks (The Body Coach) | E60
episodes.guest_title: The Body Coach
episodes.summary: The episode explores Joe Wicks' personal journey through addiction, depression, and the lasting impact of childhood trauma. Despite achieving immense professional success, Wicks candidly shares his experience of feeling lost and the ongoing efforts to address his mental health through therapy and an exploration of psychedelics. He delves into the complexities of marriage, the pervasive influence of social media on his life, and his evolving perspective on the role money plays in true happiness, highlighting that external achievements don't always resolve internal struggles.
key_highlights: Achieving ultimate goals can still leave one feeling profoundly lost
Childhood experiences and trauma deeply shape adult mental health
Therapy and psychedelics are explored as tools for mental well-being
Open discussion about the challenges and complexities of marriage
Reflecting on life's regrets and the true role of money in happiness
The pervasive influence of social media on personal life and identity
episodes.quotes: Even after achieving my biggest goals, the echoes of childhood trauma and past struggles can leave you feeling profoundly lost.

episodes.title: Lessons From 50 Of The Worlds Greatest Minds with Jake Humphrey | E59
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder, The High Performance Podcast
episodes.summary: Jake Humphrey shares personal lessons learned, reflecting on his experiences with failure and anxiety, and questioning the true nature of happiness among successful individuals. He delves into the realities of burnout, offering strategies for managing difficult people and overcoming jealousy. The episode also provides practical advice on connecting with an audience, starting a podcast, and effectively networking with high-performance individuals, emphasizing the crucial role of consistency in all endeavors. Finally, the discussion touches upon the importance of addressing gender imbalance in professional settings.
key_highlights: Reflecting on personal growth and lessons learned annually
Confronting failure and anxiety as integral to success
Examining the link between success, happiness, and burnout
Practical strategies for managing difficult individuals and jealousy
Tips for podcasting, audience engagement, and high-level networking
Emphasizing consistency as a cornerstone for achievement
Discussing the importance of addressing gender imbalance in professional spheres
episodes.quotes: True success isn't just about achievement; it's about navigating failure, understanding happiness beyond the surface, and consistently learning from every experience.

episodes.title: Eddie Hearn on Selling Matchroom For 5 Billion | E58
episodes.guest_title: Chairman of Matchroom Sport
episodes.summary: Eddie Hearn delves into the relentless drive and significant sacrifices required for his success in the sports promotion industry. He introduces his 'deathbed thinking' framework, a method for prioritizing life and career decisions by envisioning future regrets. The episode explores the challenges of maintaining work-life balance within relationships and Hearn's personal experiences with mental health. He also reflects on his relationship with social media and the downsides of celebrity, culminating in the strategic decision to sell Matchroom for a substantial sum.
key_highlights: Relentless drive and sacrifices are essential for achieving significant success
'Deathbed thinking' helps prioritize life choices by envisioning future regrets
Ambition's role in success and its impact on personal relationships
Navigating work-life balance to sustain relationships amidst demanding careers
Eddie Hearn's candid experience with mental health challenges and public perception
The complexities of social media and the burden of celebrity status
Strategic decision-making behind the multi-billion dollar sale of Matchroom
episodes.quotes: Deathbed thinking helps you prioritize what truly matters, ensuring you live without future regrets by making choices aligned with your deepest values.

episodes.title: Uncle Bob - The Long Reach of Code, Automating Programming, and Developing Coding Talent
episodes.guest_title: Software Engineer and Author, Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob)
episodes.summary: Uncle Bob argues that AI will not automate programming because programming fundamentally requires human sentience to specify the program's purpose. He contends that programmers are detail managers, filling in the gaps left by business users. He also suggests that the industry's focus on ethics and standards is misplaced, and that the real issue is the lack of a formal licensing body to ensure competence. He concludes that the best way to train programmers is through mentorship and apprenticeship.
key_highlights: AI cannot automate programming because it lacks human sentience to specify the program's purpose
Programmers are detail managers, filling in gaps left by business users
Human intuition is needed to fill in the blanks of business specifications
The industry's focus on ethics is misplaced; competence is the real issue
A programming guild structure with licensing could improve the industry
The best way to train programmers is through mentorship and apprenticeship
episodes.quotes: "In order to finally replicate programming, that machine would have to have human sentience, and we are very, very far away from that kind of a machine."

episodes.title: How She Built Her Confidence, and Then an Empire with Krissy Cela | E57
episodes.guest_title: Entrepreneur and Author
episodes.summary: Krissy Cela discusses the multifaceted journey of building an empire, emphasizing that outward success often masks internal struggles. She shares how physical activity, particularly the gym, was foundational in building her confidence, which then propelled her entrepreneurial ventures. The episode delves into the personal costs of ambition, including navigating relationship challenges, the societal pressures on women, and the often-unspoken 'bullshit' of entrepreneurship. Cela also candidly opens up about her battle with depression and the constant struggle to find work-life balance, highlighting the mental health toll of high achievement.
key_highlights: Gym training was crucial for building foundational confidence
Entrepreneurial success often comes with significant personal and relational costs
Societal pressures and gender dynamics impact women entrepreneurs
Mental health struggles like depression can accompany high achievement
Maintaining relationships and friendships is challenging for founders
The reality of entrepreneurship differs from its glamorous perception
episodes.quotes: Building an empire isn't just about the wins; it's about the hidden battles with self-doubt, depression, and the constant pressure to justify your success.

episodes.title: Scott Aaronson - Quantum Computing, Complexity, and Creativity
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Computer Science, University of Texas at Austin
episodes.summary: Scott Aaronson discusses his early life as a prodigy, his research on the Busy Beaver function, and the limitations of existing set theory. He also touches on the social dynamics of academia and the challenges of pursuing research outside of traditional academic institutions. Aaronson emphasizes the importance of imagination and the potential for new discoveries in quantum computing.
key_highlights: Becoming a world expert on a tiny problem
Quantum mechanics was not always the final word
The importance of having a good math teacher
The limitations of set theory
The importance of imagination in research
The value of being on the margins of academia
episodes.quotes: It really doesn't take that long to become the world expert on one particular tiny little problem. So try to become the world expert on something, you know, even something very, very narrow.

episodes.title: Eric Garcetti, Mayor of Los Angeles
episodes.guest_title: Mayor of Los Angeles
episodes.summary: Mayor Garcetti discusses the importance of cities as laboratories for democracy and innovation. He emphasizes the need for leaders to be both humble and fearless, and to prioritize listening over speaking. Garcetti shares his approach to leadership, which involves balancing urgent needs with long-term vision, and leading with love. He also highlights the importance of rehumanizing people and fostering human connection in an increasingly virtual world.
key_highlights: Cities are laboratories for democracy and innovation
Balance urgent needs with long-term vision
Lead with love and empathy
Rehumanize people in a virtual world
Prioritize listening over speaking
Be humble but fearless
Build strong relationships with stakeholders
episodes.quotes: The word for city in Greek has the same root as what we now use for politics. People came to cities to engage with each other, and part of that obligation of citizenship was to engage in politics.

episodes.title: Scott Young - Ultralearning, The MIT Challenge
episodes.guest_title: Author of Ultralearning
episodes.summary: Scott Young discusses the phenomenon of "miracle years" in the lives of outlier individuals like Newton and Einstein, arguing that it is largely a selection effect. He also discusses the importance of specialization, and the need to understand the specific goals of a learning project in order to optimize it. He contrasts this with the more general, broad-based learning that is often promoted, and argues that the two have different goals and outcomes.
key_highlights: Most people never have a year where they accomplish anything
Selection effect: smart people working on the right problem
Einstein's insights led to physics breakthroughs
Most people don't deserve to be on lists of geniuses
The importance of doing research before starting a project
Need to understand what success looks like
episodes.quotes: "Rewarding kind of a culture where you want to do kind of ambitious original things that um are kind of interesting and you don't know where they're going to lead."

episodes.title: She Cheated On Me and Thats Not All - Dr. Aria | E56
episodes.guest_title: Chartered Psychologist
episodes.summary: The episode features Dr. Aria recounting the profound personal story of his partner's infidelity and the subsequent emotional turmoil. He details his process of confronting and processing intense emotions, which led to a fundamental re-evaluation of his long-held beliefs about marriage and monogamy. This journey involved grappling with a significant loss of his intrinsic self-identity, ultimately guiding him to conceptualize and articulate his ideal, non-traditional relationship structure. A core insight shared is the argument that much personal unhappiness originates from conforming to societal expectations rather than pursuing authentic individual desires.
key_highlights: Personal account of infidelity and its emotional aftermath
Strategies for processing betrayal and intense feelings
Re-evaluating traditional marriage and monogamy concepts
Impact of relationship trauma on personal identity
Developing an authentic, non-conformist relationship structure
Unhappiness often stems from societal conformity
episodes.quotes: The pain of betrayal forced a re-evaluation of everything, revealing how much unhappiness stems from conforming to expectations rather than living authentically.

episodes.title: Beth Ford, President and CEO, Land O' Lakes
episodes.guest_title: President and CEO, Land O' Lakes
episodes.summary: Beth Ford, President and CEO of Land O' Lakes, shares her journey from a working-class background to leading a Fortune 200 company. She emphasizes the importance of authenticity, empathy, and understanding the needs of others, particularly in rural communities. Ford discusses the challenges and opportunities in the agriculture industry, including sustainability, food security, and the digital divide. She highlights the need for innovative incentive structures to support farmers and promote biodiversity.
key_highlights: Calmness is contagious in crisis; pump the brakes, don't jerk the wheel
Prioritize people, not just profits, in leadership
Be a self-advocate and ask for what you want
Be aware of the digital divide and its impact
Seek out opportunities to enable others' success
You can't be a mind reader, so ask
The most important career decision is who you marry
episodes.quotes: If you want something, you ask for it. And otherwise, people aren't going to guess. Nobody, I promise you, cares more about your career than you do.

episodes.title: Why We're Getting More Depressed, Anxious and Lonely | E55
episodes.guest_title: Host of The Diary of a CEO podcast, Author
episodes.summary: Steven Bartlett explores the root causes of modern depression, anxiety, and loneliness, arguing that a fundamental misunderstanding of human desires contributes to these issues. He proposes that fixing motivation loss involves understanding what truly drives individuals, not just external rewards. A key to success, he suggests, is the discipline to decline good opportunities to preserve focus for truly great ones. Bartlett also challenges conventional societal norms, questioning the universal suitability of marriage and advocating strongly against the pressure to "fit in" as a pathway to personal fulfillment and mental well-being.
key_highlights: Understanding why motivation is lost is crucial for personal progress
Modern depression and anxiety stem from a lack of self-awareness
Humans often pursue goals without truly knowing what they desire
Success requires rejecting good opportunities to focus on great ones
Societal pressure to "fit in" is detrimental to individual happiness
Traditional institutions like marriage may not be universally beneficial
episodes.quotes: Fitting In Is A Curse.

episodes.title: Charles Murray - Human Accomplishment and the Future of Liberty | The Lunar Society #10
episodes.guest_title: Political Scientist and Author, American Enterprise Institute
episodes.summary: Charles Murray discusses the nature of human accomplishment, the role of religion in Western Civilization, and the decline of social sciences. Murray argues that secular humanism lacks the staying power of religion and that the decline in religious adherence is correlated with a decline in societal values. He also discusses the importance of hard work and the cultural milieu in fostering human achievement.
key_highlights: Secular humanism lacks the staying power of religion
The importance of religion to Western civilization
Secularization is correlated with a decline in societal values
Hard work is a common theme among high achievers
The importance of cultural milieu in fostering human achievement
episodes.quotes: Secular humanism didn't have the staying power that it needs.

episodes.title: Overcoming Depression, Burnout, Anxiety and Insomnia with Dan Murray-Serter | E54
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder of Heights
episodes.summary: Dan Murray-Serter shares his personal journey through depression, burnout, anxiety, and insomnia, detailing the origins and impact of these struggles. The episode explores his experiences with psychedelics as a potential therapeutic tool and his strategies for maintaining resilience despite public scrutiny and entrepreneurial failures. He discusses the development of his supplement brand, Heights, and the importance of relationships and self-reflection in achieving well-being and happiness. The conversation delves into finding the courage to persevere and confronting existential questions about happiness and mortality.
key_highlights: Dan's personal battle with depression and its initial triggers
Exploring psychedelics as a method for mental health treatment
Strategies for overcoming burnout and managing chronic anxiety
Building resilience to persist despite failures and public opinion
The role of supplements and entrepreneurship in personal well-being
The critical impact of relationships on mental health and happiness
Confronting fears of mortality and finding contentment in life

episodes.title: Alex Tabarrok - Prizes, Prices, and Public Goods
episodes.guest_title: Bartley J. Madden Chair of Economics at the Mercatus Center
episodes.summary: Alex Tabarrok discusses the Grand Innovation Prize, which aims to incentivize the rapid development of vaccines, diagnostics, and therapeutics. He argues that speed is crucial in pandemics and that existing incentives for companies are insufficient. Tabarrok also explores the Baumol effect, explaining why prices in stagnant sectors like education and healthcare rise due to productivity gains in progressive sectors, and how this affects skilled labor.
key_highlights: Speed is crucial in pandemics, existing incentives for companies are insufficient
Grand Innovation Prize is meant to incentivize quick vaccine production
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
Social incentives are needed to speed up vaccine production
Baumol effect explains rising prices in stagnant sectors
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
Vaccine manufacturers are unwilling to build factories until vaccines are proven safe
episodes.quotes: "We want to find a way of speeding up the incentives to produce a vaccine or a diagnostic or a therapeutic."

episodes.title: I Have A Secret To Tell You... | E53
episodes.guest_title: Host of The Diary of a CEO Podcast & Entrepreneur
episodes.summary: Steven Bartlett shares a personal revelation about testing positive for an illness, using it as a catalyst for deeper self-reflection and understanding. He discusses the concept of neuroplasticity, emphasizing how intentional questioning and introspection can literally reshape one's brain and perspective. Bartlett argues that seeking fewer definitive answers and embracing more profound questions is crucial for personal growth and self-discovery. He highlights the powerful psychological link between childhood experiences of invalidation and the adult pursuit of external validation, suggesting that early wounds often dictate later behaviors.
key_highlights: Personal revelation about testing positive for an illness
Pandemic's lesson on vulnerability and self-awareness
Neuroplasticity: actively changing brain structure through thought
Prioritizing profound questions over seeking quick answers
Self-inquiry as a tool for personal development
Childhood invalidation drives adult validation-seeking behaviors
episodes.quotes: The thing that invalided you when you were younger, will be the thing you seek validation from as an adult.

episodes.title: Rosalind Brewer, COO, Starbucks
episodes.guest_title: Chief Operating Officer, Starbucks
episodes.summary: Rosalind Brewer shares her journey from a chemistry degree to leading Starbucks operations. She emphasizes the importance of integrity, building trust, and using one's voice to drive change. Brewer recounts a pivotal moment when she realized the need to bring her whole self to work, and how the company's response to the Philadelphia incident shaped her leadership. She encourages students to embrace their unique perspectives and to be a voice for change.
key_highlights: Integrity is a core value in leadership
Build trust with your team
Use your voice to drive change
Bring your whole self to work
Focus on impact, not just titles
Meet your employees where they are
Be an adept glass-cutter
episodes.quotes: "Those glass ceilings are real, but Roz Brewer has become an adept glass-cutter. She has walked the same road as many of you and with relentless determination."

episodes.title: Caleb Watney - America's Innovation Engine
episodes.guest_title: Director of Innovation Policy, Progressive Policy Institute
episodes.summary: Caleb Watney argues that America's innovation engine is slowing due to three trends: declining high-skill immigration, a weakening university system, and the erosion of industrial clusters. He contends that while online communication platforms can partially replicate the benefits of physical agglomeration, they cannot fully substitute for the spontaneous idea-sharing and cultural context that arise from in-person interactions. Watney suggests that the US should focus on policies that attract and retain top talent, promote basic research, and foster a culture of risk-taking and experimentation.
key_highlights: The US's innovation engine is slowing due to declining high-skill immigration
Remote work can't fully replace physical agglomeration for innovation
China's innovation strategy is talent acquisition, not just R&D spending
The H-1B visa program is poorly structured for attracting top talent
The US needs to promote a culture of risk-taking and experimentation
The government should make data more accessible to foster innovation
episodes.quotes: "When you have all of those people in a geographical region where they're running into each other spontaneously and they're sharing ideas and they're having long conversations...that they wouldn't otherwise be able to replicate simply online, that ends up leading to more creativity."

episodes.title: Robin Hanson - The Long View and The Elephant in the Brain
episodes.guest_title: Research Associate, Future of Humanity Institute, Oxford University
episodes.summary: Robin Hanson discusses the lack of long-term planning in society, arguing that it's not just a matter of short-sightedness, but a consequence of the fact that optimizing for the long term is often ineffectual or even counterproductive given the inherent unpredictability of the future. He suggests that the most powerful institutions today are not necessarily the ones that thought about the long-term future in the past, and that the true unit of selection is not the individual but the organization. Hanson also argues that the lack of clear signals about long-term orientation makes it difficult to identify and reward individuals who are truly committed to the future.
key_highlights: Optimizing for the long term is often ineffectual or even counterproductive due to unpredictability
The most powerful institutions aren't necessarily long-term planners
Individual genes can't save resources for themselves, only for the whole creature
Human discount rates are influenced by the fact that children only share half our genes
The most important thing is to promote the thing that embodies the long view
episodes.quotes: "If it's unpredictable, then optimizing should take that into account."

episodes.title: Jason Crawford - The Roots of Progress & the History of Technology
episodes.guest_title: Writer at Roots of Progress
episodes.summary: Jason Crawford discusses the history of technology and the philosophy of progress. He argues that progress is not automatic and requires a conscious effort to protect and nurture it. He also notes that the linear model of innovation is oversimplified, as science and technology often influence each other in complex ways. Crawford emphasizes the importance of prescriptive optimism, which is the determination to act to create a better future, regardless of the odds.
key_highlights: Progress requires a conscious effort to protect and nurture it
The linear model of innovation is oversimplified
Science and technology influence each other in complex ways
Prescriptive optimism is key to creating a better future
Progress is not automatic or inevitable
Progress studies is a school of thought, not a new field
episodes.quotes: Progress is not automatic or inevitable, it doesn't just keep going with its own momentum, it can slow down, maybe it's running into friction, let's take a look at that.

episodes.title: Matjaž Leonardis - Science, Identity and Probability
episodes.guest_title: Researcher
episodes.summary: Leonardis argues that identifying too strongly with "science" can be counterproductive, as it can lead to a focus on whether one's work is "scientific" rather than on the pursuit of truth. He also discusses the Popper-Miller theorem, which shows that evidence always decreases the probability of the inductive part of a theory. He suggests that the Enlightenment narrative is a fiction, and that people are naturally interested in everything.
key_highlights: Identifying too strongly with "science" can be counterproductive
People are naturally interested in everything
The Enlightenment narrative is a fiction
The Popper-Miller theorem shows that evidence decreases the probability of the inductive part of a theory
There is no such thing as an unsolved problem
episodes.quotes: "Polymath is not something you learn to be, it's something you unlearn to be."

episodes.title: Tyler Cowen - The Great Reset
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Tyler Cowen discusses his 2017 book, *The Complacent Class*, and its prediction of a "Great Reset" — a crisis that would disrupt the complacency of American society. He reflects on how the COVID-19 pandemic has fulfilled this prediction, highlighting the failure of the regulatory state and the executive branch to respond effectively. Cowen also explores the potential for a burst of dynamism in biomedicine and the challenges of maintaining progress given the inherent limitations of human nature and the ever-present threat of war.
key_highlights: The Great Reset arrived sooner than Cowen anticipated
The American regulatory state has failed to handle the pandemic
Biomedicine is poised for a burst of innovation
Complacency has finally caught up with us
Predictions should be conditional
The Fed and Supreme Court have been quite good
episodes.quotes: "At some point, our country will face an immediate crisis, and there won't be the resources or more fundamentally the flexibility to handle it."

episodes.title: Bryan Caplan - Nurturing Orphaned Ideas, Education, and UBI
episodes.guest_title: Professor of Economics, George Mason University
episodes.summary: Bryan Caplan discusses the importance of disagreeableness for intellectual progress, arguing that it's more effective than charm for truth-seeking. He defends his radical views on open borders and critiques the conventional wisdom surrounding education, arguing that it's largely signaling. Caplan also expresses skepticism about UBI, suggesting that it's based on a flawed understanding of the causes of poverty, which he attributes to bad government policies and individual irresponsibility.
key_highlights: Charm is useful for gaining power, but disagreeableness is more effective for truth-seeking
Most of what is learned in school is not broadly useful
Open borders are a moral imperative
Nuclear weapons have increased peace
The poor are often held in poverty by bad government policies
Personal responsibility is a major cause of poverty
episodes.quotes: "I've got enough controversial statements under my belt that people focus on that."

episodes.title: Stephen Curry, 2X MVP & 3X NBA Champion, Golden State Warriors
episodes.guest_title: 2X MVP & 3X NBA Champion, Golden State Warriors
episodes.summary: Stephen Curry shares his journey from Davidson College to the NBA, emphasizing the importance of family, faith, and a strong work ethic. He discusses the transition from a shaky Warriors team to a dynasty, highlighting the value of communication and humility in leadership. Curry also touches on his venture into media and investing, aiming to inspire the next generation through storytelling and creating opportunities for underserved communities.
key_highlights: Family provides a sense of foundation, no matter what life throws
Don't take yourself too seriously in your job
Embrace differences in personality on a team
Don't lose confidence, even with roadblocks
Stay educated and be authentic
Don't be afraid to share the spotlight
It's important to have a village around you
episodes.quotes: I'm only as good as the people around me. I try to deflect as much as possible to my team, because I know that I have power because of who I get to roll with every single day on the court.

episodes.title: David Solomon, CEO, Goldman Sachs
episodes.guest_title: CEO, Goldman Sachs
episodes.summary: David Solomon shares his career journey, emphasizing the importance of embracing change and the role of luck. He discusses Goldman Sachs' commitment to sustainability and financial inclusion, and the need for a more open, accessible culture. Solomon also reflects on his personal journey, including his passion for music and how it has shaped his leadership style.
key_highlights: Value people, capital, and technology
Embrace change and be adaptable
Focus on sustainability and financial inclusion
Prioritize a modern, open, and accessible culture
Balance work and personal life
Be authentic and bring your whole self to work
episodes.quotes: “If you wake up two-thirds of the time and you’re really happy with your job, you’re really lucky and you’re in a really good place.”

episodes.title: Rose Marcario, President & CEO, Patagonia
episodes.guest_title: President & CEO, Patagonia
episodes.summary: Rose Marcario shares her journey from private equity to leading Patagonia, driven by a desire to align her work with her values. She describes Patagonia's commitment to environmentalism, its unique workforce policies like subsidized childcare, and its activism against environmental deregulation. Marcario emphasizes the importance of curiosity, self-examination, and taking a stand for what's right, even when it's difficult, and how business leaders should consider the planet as a key stakeholder.
key_highlights: Align work with personal values for a more meaningful career
Fearless leadership is essential for making difficult decisions
Prioritize quality over quantity in product development
Business must evolve to consider multiple stakeholders
Environmental activism is a core part of Patagonia's brand
Onsite childcare promotes gender parity in management
episodes.quotes: You don't get points for holding back.

episodes.title: Stewart Butterfield, Cofounder and CEO of Slack
episodes.guest_title: Cofounder and CEO of Slack
episodes.summary: Stewart Butterfield shares his journey from philosophy student to tech entrepreneur, discussing the importance of starting early and the challenges of building a company with a new concept. He emphasizes the need for a strong team and a clear vision, and the importance of being thoughtful and considerate of others. He also shares his experience of selling Flickr to Yahoo and the lessons he learned from that experience.
key_highlights: Start early, even if you don't know where you're going
Build a strong team
Have a clear vision
Be thoughtful and considerate of others
Don't see people as instruments or obstacles
episodes.quotes: The real fundamental challenge of leadership is the same as the fundamental challenge of just being a human being.

episodes.title: Ruth Zukerman, Co-Founder of SoulCycle and Flywheel Sports
episodes.guest_title: Co-Founder, SoulCycle and Flywheel Sports
episodes.summary: Ruth Zukerman, co-founder of SoulCycle and Flywheel Sports, shares her journey from ballet to boutique fitness. She emphasizes the importance of customer service and creating a personal connection with clients, drawing parallels to her experience as a mother. Zukerman also discusses the challenges of partnership, the importance of self-knowledge, and the need to be vulnerable as a leader. She reflects on the impact of her difficult childhood and how therapy helped her discover her voice.
key_highlights: Dance as a way to express herself without words
Importance of therapy and self-discovery
Customer service and personal connection are key
Importance of knowing yourself when choosing partners
Empathy in moderation is important for leadership
Everything happens for a reason
No wrong decisions, just learning opportunities
episodes.quotes: It made sense that I got so addicted to dancing because it was it became my way of expressing myself without words, without a voice.

episodes.title: Satya Nadella, CEO of Microsoft
episodes.guest_title: CEO, Microsoft
episodes.summary: Satya Nadella discusses his childhood in India, his parents' influence, and the importance of empathy in leadership. He shares a story about his high school cricket captain, who gave him the ball back after a bad performance, teaching him the value of confidence and humility. Nadella emphasizes that Microsoft's mission is to empower others, not to be cool. He highlights the importance of a growth mindset, and the need for leaders to create a culture that supports learning and innovation.
key_highlights: Microsoft's mission is to make others cool, not to be cool
Importance of confidence and humility in leadership
Team sports teach valuable lessons about leadership and collaboration
Need to balance growth with responsibility to society
The best leaders have high empathy and a growth mindset
episodes.quotes: The two of them, I think, growing up in Hyderabad in India in the late 70s, early 80s, gave me in spite of all of that a lot of confidence because to him, he felt like look, it's a marathon, you'll catch up, this is not that hard.

episodes.title: Marcos Galperin, MBA ’99, Founder and CEO of MercadoLibre, on Taking Risks and Being Resilient
episodes.guest_title: Co-Founder and CEO, MercadoLibre
episodes.summary: Marcos Galperin, CEO of MercadoLibre, shares his journey of building the company in Latin America. He emphasizes the importance of building strong relationships, having a long-term vision, and being resilient in the face of challenges. Galperin also highlights the significance of adapting to local market conditions and building a strong team with a shared sense of purpose.
key_highlights: Start with a success case to inspire others
Build relationships
Have a long-term vision
Be resilient
Adapt to local market conditions
Build a strong team
episodes.quotes: They got tired of telling me that my idea was not going to work.

episodes.title: Ruth Porat, CFO at Alphabet and Google
episodes.guest_title: CFO, Alphabet and Google
episodes.summary: Ruth Porat discusses her career path, from her early beginnings to Morgan Stanley to Alphabet and Google. She emphasizes the importance of education, which was instilled in her by her father, who escaped Austria during World War II. She also shares how she learned to be flexible and adjust as the world changes, and how she learned to use her voice at the right time. She stresses the importance of having a sponsor and being a sponsor to others.
key_highlights: Education is your passport for life
Gratitude to be in a country with freedom
Importance of having a career
Be flexible and adjust as the world changes
Use your voice at the right time
Importance of having a sponsor
Importance of analytical rigor
episodes.quotes: I would rather die an educated man. | The world is small and life is long. | Anchor everything in data and the rest will follow.

episodes.title: Ray Dalio, Founder and Chairman, Bridgewater Associates
episodes.guest_title: Founder and Chairman, Bridgewater Associates
episodes.summary: Ray Dalio shares his journey from a Long Island paperboy to the founder of Bridgewater Associates. He emphasizes the importance of thoughtful disagreement and radical transparency in building a successful organization. Dalio discusses his early investment in the stock market at age 12 and his decision to pursue commodity trading over equity trading. He also reflects on the importance of learning from failures and the value of having a strong sense of community.
key_highlights: Embrace thoughtful disagreement and radical transparency
Importance of having a strong sense of community
Learn from failures and turn them into principles
Value the process of personal evolution
Prioritize meaningful work and relationships
Balance audacity with humility
Understand history to anticipate future trends
episodes.quotes: “The greatest tragedy of mankind is people who hold opinions in their mind that they’re attached to that are wrong, that they could so easily stress test.”

episodes.title: Phil Knight, MBA ’62, Co-founder and Chairman Emeritus, Nike
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder and Chairman Emeritus, Nike
episodes.summary: Phil Knight shares his journey building Nike, starting with his thesis at Stanford Business School about importing Japanese running shoes. He emphasizes the importance of having a niche and a passion for what you're building, and the need to adapt leadership styles as a company grows. Knight also reflects on the importance of a strong company culture, and how Nike's early culture was built on a shared belief and a willingness to challenge the status quo.
key_highlights: Find a niche that fits your passion
Good shoes are made by good factories
Hero leadership is not autocratic
You can't be afraid of offending people
You have to take a stand on something
It starts with the product
You need to believe
episodes.quotes: It doesn't matter how many people hate your brand, as long as enough people love it.

episodes.title: Michael Moritz, Partner, Sequoia Capital
episodes.guest_title: Partner, Sequoia Capital
episodes.summary: Michael Moritz shares his journey from a boy in Wales to a journalist and eventually a venture capitalist. He discusses the importance of having a long-term vision, and the value of a broad-based humanities education for success in technology. He also emphasizes the importance of listening and having a stable team for enduring organizations.
key_highlights: Wanting the ball to come in your direction
The importance of a broad-based humanities education
The importance of being a clear communicator
The importance of having a long-term vision
The importance of having a stable team
The importance of listening
episodes.quotes: The only thing that separated him and me on the rugby field was when he took to the field, he wanted the ball to come in his direction, and I of course wanted the reverse. I never wanted to see it come in my direction.

episodes.title: Dara Khosrowshahi, CEO, Uber
episodes.guest_title: CEO, Uber
episodes.summary: Dara Khosrowshahi shares his journey from Iran to the US as a child, fleeing the revolution and seeking asylum. He emphasizes the importance of family and education in his assimilation. Khosrowshahi discusses his career path, highlighting the influence of mentor Barry Diller, and his decision to join Uber, driven by the opportunity to make a significant impact. He stresses the importance of transparency, customer obsession, and building a diverse leadership team.
key_highlights: Children assimilate more easily than adults
Family provides a constant source of support
Sports can be a tool for assimilation
Always answer "no" if you don't understand
Bet on people, not just companies
Go to the source for information
Success imprints more forcefully than failure
episodes.quotes: It's about doing something great. | I want your portfolio to be me. | There are very few times in your life when you can change the world. | We lost, they won, next.

episodes.title: Diane Von Fürstenberg, Founder & Chairman, DVF
episodes.guest_title: Founder & Chairman, DVF
episodes.summary: Diane Von Fürstenberg discusses her career, starting with an apprenticeship in a factory. She emphasizes the importance of being open to opportunities, even if they don't seem glamorous. She describes how getting pregnant and marrying a prince led to her starting her own business, and how her mother's strength and resilience influenced her. She also shares her thoughts on the importance of character, and how to be a strong and independent woman.
key_highlights: Be open to opportunities, even if they don't seem glamorous
You don't know what you're going to do
The dress made me
The most important relationship is with yourself
You can never be a victim
The power of color
Women have to stick together
episodes.quotes: The most important relationship you have is the one you have with yourself. You have yourself as a refuge, as a shelter, and no matter what happens, you always have yourself.

episodes.title: Oscar Munoz, President & CEO, United Airlines, on Knowing Yourself
episodes.guest_title: President & CEO, United Airlines
episodes.summary: Oscar Munoz shares his journey from a working-class family in Southern California to leading United Airlines, emphasizing the importance of knowing oneself and building strong relationships. He recounts a pivotal moment with a high school counselor that led him to college and discusses the challenges of integrating diverse cultures after the United-Continental merger. Munoz stresses the value of a caring culture and the responsibility of leaders to advocate for social causes.
key_highlights: Environment and upbringing shape who you are as a person
Latino heritage emphasizes family, humility, and loyalty
Concept of entitlement was not strong in his world
Being first-generation undergrad brings big responsibility
Better to be principled than just profitable
Importance of building trust with employees
You have to know yourself, really
episodes.quotes: "I think environment, how you grew up, who you grow up with, has a meaningful impact on who you are as a person."

episodes.title: Ken Frazier, CEO of Merck & Co, Inc.
episodes.guest_title: CEO of Merck & Co, Inc.
episodes.summary: Ken Frazier shares his journey from death penalty lawyer to CEO of Merck, emphasizing the role of opportunity and access in shaping his career. He recounts his experience with school desegregation, which instilled a deep awareness of the opportunity gap. Frazier stresses the importance of business leaders having a broader purpose beyond shareholder value, focusing on contributing to humanity. He also notes the importance of having a board with diverse perspectives and the need to be willing to challenge the status quo.
key_highlights: Opportunity gap exists in society
Social engineers engaged in school desegregation
Business education has real value
There is serendipity in everyone's career path
The importance of giving back to the community
There are two fundamental metrics: people helped and amount of help
Focus on what's right, not what people think
episodes.quotes: What other people think about you is none of your damn business.

episodes.title: Sara Blakely, Founder and CEO, Spanx
episodes.guest_title: Founder and CEO, Spanx
episodes.summary: Sara Blakely, founder of Spanx, shares her entrepreneurial journey from selling fax machines to building a billion-dollar company. She emphasizes the importance of intuition over data, the power of vulnerability, and the need to embrace humor in business. Blakely also discusses her commitment to empowering women and the importance of creating a culture where employees are not afraid to fail.
key_highlights: Start small, think big, scale fast
Be willing to look stupid
Trust your intuition
Hire your weaknesses
Use humor to move through pain
episodes.quotes: I feel like success just makes you more of who you already were. I feel like money and success just does that.

episodes.title: Stephen Harper, 22nd Prime Minister of Canada
episodes.guest_title: 22nd Prime Minister of Canada
episodes.summary: Former Canadian Prime Minister Stephen Harper discusses his career in politics and leadership. Harper explains how he never intended to be a politician, but rather became interested in policy and economics. He also shares that he made it a point to consult with every major sector of Canadian society when making trade deals. Harper also shares that he believes that leaders should be respected, and that any leader should love their country.
key_highlights: Never intended to be a politician
Importance of economic policy
Importance of public support
The need to respect opponents
Importance of understanding interests
Importance of incrementalism in policy
Importance of loving one's country
episodes.quotes: I always felt that I could lose more votes on answering that question than on any serious issue of policy. I will take any one of them. I'm desperate at this point.

episodes.title: Ginni Rometty, Chairman, President, and CEO of IBM
episodes.guest_title: Chairman, President, and CEO of IBM
episodes.summary: Ginni Rometty shares her career journey, starting in the automobile industry and moving to tech. She emphasizes the importance of passion in one's work and the need to continually reinvent oneself. Rometty discusses IBM's commitment to responsible stewardship of technology, particularly in areas like AI and blockchain, and the company's focus on skills development and inclusion.
key_highlights: Importance of passion in one's work
Continually reinventing oneself
Importance of purpose
Technology should be used with responsibility
Importance of skills development
Importance of inclusion and diversity
IBM's commitment to open innovation
episodes.quotes: "Life is too short. You've got to keep moving till you find what that is. I could only see what it wasn't."

episodes.title: Jeffrey Katzenberg, Co-founder and Former CEO of Dreamworks Animation
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder and Former CEO, Dreamworks Animation
episodes.summary: Jeffrey Katzenberg discusses his career journey, from working for Mayor John Lindsay to co-founding Dreamworks. He emphasizes the importance of adaptability and continuous learning, sharing his experience of starting over after being fired from Disney. Katzenberg highlights the value of strong partnerships, citing his collaborations with Steven Spielberg and David Geffen, and stresses the significance of courage in taking risks and pursuing original ideas in the entertainment industry.

Value your voice and your vote
Work smarter, not harder
Embrace failure and take risks
The truth will find you
Mentorship is invaluable
Be a builder and an operator

episodes.title: Tom Steyer, MBA '83, Founder and President of NextGen America
episodes.guest_title: Founder and President, NextGen America
episodes.summary: Tom Steyer reflects on his time as a GSB student, his investment career, and his shift to environmentalism and political activism. He discusses the importance of investing in people and the need for broader democracy to address the challenges facing the United States. Steyer emphasizes the importance of having a meaningful life and contributing to society, and he shares his perspective on the role of money in politics.

Interpersonal dynamics class was a chance to see how people interact
Competitive advantage on a national level is important
The importance of having a different perspective
Measure the results of your investments
The importance of internal communication
The importance of having a meaningful life

episodes.title: Chamath Palihapitiya, Founder and CEO Social Capital, on Money as an Instrument of Change
episodes.guest_title: Founder and CEO, Social Capital
episodes.summary: Chamath Palihapitiya discusses his journey from a welfare-dependent childhood to becoming a successful venture capitalist. He emphasizes that money is an instrument of change, and that his goal is to amass capital to reallocate it towards his worldview. He encourages the audience to be good copiers, but to also develop their own unique perspectives. He also shares his belief that the current social media landscape is eroding society and that it is important to be aware of the psychological effects of these tools.
key_highlights: Money is a tool to empower oneself to do what one wants
Empathize with people who are like you
Don't covet money, use it to empower others
Be a good copier, but be honest
It's powerful to say "I don't know"
Be aware of the psychological effects of social media
Be anonymously generous
episodes.quotes: I feel very lucky because I don't think if you ask my sisters, they got to the same place that I did. Um, but I ended up not coveting it, and I found it to be something that I could use to really empower myself to do the things that I wanted to do.

episodes.title: Jamie Dimon, Chairman, President, and CEO of JPMorgan Chase
episodes.guest_title: Chairman, President, and CEO of JPMorgan Chase
episodes.summary: Jamie Dimon discusses his career path, leadership style, and views on the financial industry. He recounts his decision to leave Harvard Business School to work for Sandy Weill, emphasizing the value of being part of an organization. He stresses the importance of honesty, transparency, and strong character in leadership. Dimon also shares insights on the impact of technology on financial services and the need for competitive tax reform in the US.

Bureaucracy and politics destroy companies
Character is a sine qua non
You can have it all, but not all at the same time
Be a patriot and help your country
You should be loyal to the institution
Be careful about who you put in charge
episodes.quotes: “When you meet men and women our age at JP Morgan today, what leadership characteristics are you looking for in them? Character is a sine qua non. And by character, I also mean that they tell the whole truth, nothing but the truth.”

episodes.title: Sheryl Sandberg, COO of Facebook, on Using Your Voice For Good
episodes.guest_title: COO, Facebook
episodes.summary: Sheryl Sandberg shares her career journey, emphasizing the importance of taking opportunities on "rocket ships" and not planning a straight line. She discusses the value of building relationships and contributing to a company's mission. Sandberg also touches on the challenges women face in leadership, including societal biases and the need for supportive partners. She encourages the audience to be authentic and to use their voices to make a difference.

Find the rocket ship that matters to you
Invest in yourself and others' success
Prioritize process over substance in new roles
Long-run dream, short-run plan
Be authentic, not packaged
Be ambitious for the women in your life
episodes.quotes: "When you get offered a seat on a rocket ship, you don't ask what seat."

episodes.title: Mary Barra, MBA '90, Chairman and CEO of General Motors, on Achieving Results with Integrity
episodes.guest_title: Chairman and CEO, General Motors

She emphasizes the need for a collaborative environment and the value of diverse perspectives. Barra also stresses the importance of transparency and acting with integrity, especially during a crisis. She encourages young people to broaden their studies and to find a job they love.

People are still going to need to move from point A to point B
Focus on long product cycles and lean operations
Move from a hardware-centric world to a software-focused one
Understand how to create value for the customer
Provide new experiences that are better for the customer
To change behaviors, you have to be collaborative

episodes.title: Eric Schmidt, Executive Chairman, Alphabet Inc.: Just Say "Yes"
episodes.guest_title: Executive Chairman, Alphabet Inc.

He emphasizes that while material needs are being met, conflicts around religion, nationalism, and personal issues persist. He encourages students to embrace personal and economic freedom, to be grateful for their opportunities, and to focus on serving others.  He advises students to say "yes" to new experiences, to be curious, and to develop their own luck.

Entering an age of abundance
Material things in great supply
Religious, nationalistic, personal conflicts will persist
Personal freedom and economic freedom are key
Turn off Twitter to focus
Brush your teeth, you'll need them

episodes.title: Debra Lee, BET Chairman and CEO, on Leading a Company and a Community
episodes.guest_title: Chairman and CEO, BET Networks

She recounts the challenges of transitioning from a legal background to a leadership role, highlighting the need to develop management skills and a clear vision. Lee shares anecdotes from her career, including producing a tribute concert for the Obamas at the White House, and emphasizes the need to respect, reflect, and elevate one's audience.

Leadership requires developing a clear vision and management skills
Community leadership is integral to a company's success
Respect, reflect, and elevate your audience
Embrace the power of media to educate and inspire
Don't be afraid to step off the fast track
Work should be fun

episodes.title: Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel on Policy-Making & Negotiation
episodes.guest_title: Former Mayor of Chicago
episodes.summary: Former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel discusses the importance of public service and the need for leaders to be both idealistic and ruthless. He shares his approach to negotiation, emphasizing the need to understand the other party's wins and to be flexible on the means while staying true to the end goal. Emanuel also reflects on the challenges of being a political leader, including the importance of taking political hits for the greater good and the need to build a strong team.
key_highlights: Chicago has the lowest cost of living of the 10 biggest US cities
Chicago is the #1 city for female entrepreneurs
Leaders need to be both idealistic and ruthless
Find out what the other side needs to declare victory
Don't confuse means with ends
Build a strong team
episodes.quotes: "I always try to start when we negotiate something, sit at the table with my staff and write out to me their wins and the ones we can afford to give them."

episodes.title: LinkedIn CEO Jeff Weiner on Compassionate Management
episodes.guest_title: Executive Chairman, LinkedIn
episodes.summary: Jeff Weiner discusses LinkedIn's response to the 2017 executive order on immigration, emphasizing employee safety and well-being. He shares LinkedIn's vision to create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce, irrespective of origin or belief. He highlights the importance of balancing personal beliefs with representing the company, and shares how LinkedIn supports refugees in finding work, and has accelerated the launch of the Welcome Talent program in the US.
key_highlights: Well-being and safety of employees is paramount
Consider audience, constituencies, and objectives when addressing issues
Create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce
Balance personal beliefs with representing the company as CEO
Innovation at the exclusion of strong building blocks can be detrimental
Managing compassionately involves understanding others' perspectives
Strive for a culture of transformation, not just innovation
episodes.quotes: This country, the United States, was founded on a first principle and belief that all people should have an unalienable right to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.

episodes.title: Marissa Mayer on Life and Leadership Lessons
episodes.guest_title: Co-founder, Lumi Labs

She emphasizes the importance of surrounding oneself with smart people and taking risks, recounting her decision to join a small startup (Google) over more secure offers. Mayer also shares her approach to leadership, prioritizing the well-being of her children and the importance of being present in the moment.

Surround yourself with interesting and smart people
Take risks, specialize later
Focus on the child's perspective
Make a choice and commit to making it great
Technology companies reinvent themselves through acquisitions
Find a way to cut through the noise

episodes.guest_title: Co-founder & General Partner, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
episodes.summary: Marc Andreessen discusses the importance of intellectual curiosity and lifelong learning, emphasizing the value of history and the need to challenge assumptions. He shares his perspective on the venture capital industry, highlighting the shift towards a more competitive landscape and the need for a "slugging percentage mentality." He also touches on the importance of creating a culture of curiosity and learning in organizations, and the challenges of balancing action with thoughtful reflection.

Embrace a "slugging percentage mentality" in venture capital
There's so much more to learn from the past
Be ruthlessly open-minded and challenge assumptions
There is a shortage of new technology in the world
Don't self-identify with beliefs; treat them as abstract objects
It's important to have perspective of people who have it harder

episodes.title: Hank Paulson, 74th U.S. Secretary of the Treasury: "It’s the People Skills that Matter"
episodes.guest_title: 74th U.S. Secretary of the Treasury

He recounts his experience as Treasury Secretary during the 2008 financial crisis, highlighting the difficulty of gaining public support for unpopular but necessary measures. Paulson advises students to prioritize personal growth and finding work that aligns with their values, rather than solely focusing on career engineering. He underscores the significance of building trust and understanding in cross-cultural collaborations, particularly with China.

Interpersonal skills are crucial for effective leadership
Don't be afraid to admit mistakes and change course
Build trust and understanding in cross-cultural collaborations
Prioritize personal growth and align work with values
Balance career ambitions with personal life
Embrace change and be wary of career engineering
episodes.quotes: You can't ask the firm to tell you how many hours a week you're going to work. If you're working 50, how about 55, how about 60, how about 65? You have to learn to say no. You have to plan your own life.

episodes.title: Indra Nooyi, PepsiCo Chairman and CEO & Doug McMillon, Walmart President and CEO
episodes.guest_title: PepsiCo Chairman and CEO & Walmart President and CEO
episodes.summary: Indra Nooyi and Doug McMillon discuss the challenges of leading large companies in a rapidly changing world. Nooyi emphasizes the importance of design thinking and the need to connect with consumers on an emotional level, not just through utility. McMillon highlights the need to balance short-term demands with long-term vision, and the importance of staying true to core values. Nooyi shares her approach to change management, which involves helping employees understand the reasons behind the changes and empowering them to contribute to the process.

Leaders must be lifelong students, reading widely
Balance short-term demands with long-term vision
Stay true to core values, even when facing external pressures
Embrace change, but respect the company's heritage
Connect with consumers on an emotional level
Actively shape culture, don't just be a custodian
episodes.quotes: Design is something that you embed into a product or a service or an offering that romances the consumer and draws them to the shelf or to the product or to the service or whatever it is.

episodes.guest_title: Founder & CEO, Thrive Global

She also talks about the importance of being a platform for others, and how she created a civil environment at the Huffington Post. She stresses the need to prioritize well-being and sleep, and how it can improve productivity.

Failure is a stepping stone to success
Always be all in
Prioritize your health and sleep
Create a civil environment
Elevate the online conversation
Reimagine journalism

episodes.guest_title: Former President, Intel

She also discusses the challenges of promoting diversity in large organizations and the importance of knowing what is safe and good in a world that is less physical.

Mentorship is a two-way street; consider what you offer
It's dangerous to follow a strategy you don't believe in
You need frameworks and tools to think
It's important to know what's safe and good
Being a CEO is about setting strategy and creating a leadership environment
You have to be able to fail

episodes.title: American Airlines CEO Doug Parker: Go Take Risks

Relationships are key to success
Treat everyone with respect and dignity

Be a team player
Communication is key during times of crisis
It's important to adapt to change

Community means caring and respect
Spend more than 50% of your waking hours at work

Be emotionally engaged with your coworkers
Raise the bar of expected behavior
Intentionality is fundamental

episodes.title: Former Manchester United Manager Sir Alex Ferguson: Practice, Practice, Practice

Communication and recognition are vital for team success
Trust your instincts in decision-making
Never allow complacency to creep in
Acknowledge and address the human side of players
Focus on building a strong foundation
Always be honest with your players
episodes.quotes: "Practice makes you a real footballer."

episodes.title: Earvin "Magic" Johnson: Understand Your Customers and Over-Deliver
episodes.guest_title: Chairman & CEO, Magic Johnson Enterprises
episodes.summary: Earvin "Magic" Johnson discusses how the principles of focus, strategy, and discipline from basketball apply to business. He shares the importance of having mentors, and how his relationship with Dr. Jerry Buss of the Lakers helped him transition to business. Johnson emphasizes knowing your customer and over-delivering to them, even if it means changing your product. He also stresses the importance of resilience and not letting others define you.
key_highlights: Focus, strategy, and discipline are key in both basketball and business
Mentors are critical to success
Know your customer and over-deliver
Be ready to adapt and adjust
Don't let others define you
Be a perfectionist
Take advantage of your platform
episodes.quotes: "The same principles really apply from being a basketball player as well as a CEO. When you think about focus, strategy, discipline, I'm still that same guy that has those same principles as a CEO."

episodes.title: Former NYTimes Executive Editor Jill Abramson on Resilience in Leadership
episodes.guest_title: Former Executive Editor, The New York Times
episodes.summary: Jill Abramson shares her journey from a childhood steeped in reverence for the New York Times to becoming its first female executive editor. She emphasizes the importance of resilience, drawing from her own experience of being fired from the Times and her father's encouragement to persevere. Abramson also reflects on the changing media landscape, highlighting the need for quality journalism and the challenges of balancing business interests with journalistic integrity.
key_highlights: The New York Times took the place of religion in her household
Importance of being true to your values
Investigative reporting is lonely work
Passion for ambitious stories is key
The importance of listening more and talking less
Need to balance business interests with journalistic integrity
Resilience is key to success
episodes.quotes: The New York Times took the place of religion. If the Times said it, it was the absolute truth.

episodes.title: Vinod Khosla, MBA '80: Failure does not matter. Success matters.
episodes.guest_title: Founder, Khosla Ventures
episodes.summary: Vinod Khosla, founder of Sun Microsystems and Khosla Ventures, discusses his leadership and investment philosophy. He shares a story of his early days at Sun, when he refused to leave a client's lobby until he secured a meeting, emphasizing the importance of making what you believe happen. Khosla advises against listening to customers when creating innovative products and encourages a willingness to fail, as success is what ultimately matters. He emphasizes the importance of having a strong belief system and internal compass.
key_highlights: Make what you believe happen
Customers aren't always right
Don't always listen to customers
Failure does not matter, success does
Don't be afraid to fail
Have a strong belief system
Have an internal compass
episodes.quotes: Whatever I believe, I should make happen. It's that simple. And if a customer is making a mistake, the customer isn't always right, trust me. If you want to do great products, don't listen to your customers by the way.

episodes.title: Mitt Romney on Leadership: Know Your Values
episodes.guest_title: Former Governor of Massachusetts and U.S. Senator for Utah
episodes.summary: Mitt Romney discusses his career path, his decision to leave Bain Capital to lead the Salt Lake City Olympics, and his run for President. He emphasizes the importance of having something bigger than yourself to believe in, and the value of real-world experience before entering politics. He shares an anecdote of inviting a group of Boy Scouts to his house for a pool party while running Bain Capital, highlighting his commitment to community.
key_highlights: The currency in life is the people you love
Have something bigger than work
Don't compete with your parents
Service is a core value
Follow the career path you enjoy
The goal is to lift more people
Conservative principles help people see rising incomes
episodes.quotes: I believe that God loves all of us, and I believe he loves us as you would love your children. Some are doing naughty things, some are doing nice things, but you love them all.

episodes.title: HP CEO Meg Whitman on Integrity & Courage in Leadership
episodes.guest_title: CEO, Hewlett-Packard
episodes.summary: Meg Whitman shares her journey from losing a gubernatorial race to becoming the CEO of HP.
